stories.title: Richland Capital Converting Tampa Offices to Warehouse Facility
stories.excerpt: Richland Capital Holdings broke ground on Lakeview Corporate Park, a new 310,000-square-foot logistics space, at 6700 Lakeview Center Drive. The Tampa Bay Business Journal reports the project will redevelop a three-story office building into two rear-load distribution buildings designed to serve a wide range of logistics, manufacturing, distribution and light industrial users seeking immediate access to the region’s transportation network. Lakeview Corporate Park will include a 106,375-square-foot building and a 203,320-square-foot building with a shared truck court featuring 32-foot clear heights. There will also be opportunity for build-to-suit and speculative office options, as well as divided configurations to accommodate a variety of tenant sizes. Richland Capital Holdings purchased the property in 2023 for $10.97 million. The office building was constructed in 1984. JLL’s Peter Cecora, John Dunphy, and Olivia Brock Martino, will oversee leasing on behalf of Richland Capital Holdings. Connect Industrial West | August 20 | Irvine, CAOn August 20, Connect Industrial West will bring together the owners, investors, developers, brokers, lenders, and occupiers driving industrial real estate across the Western U.S. Join top decision-makers for an afternoon of market insights, dealmaking opportunities, and networking with the industry’s most influential players.  Register now: www.connectindustrialwest2026.com The post Richland Capital Converting Tampa Offices to Warehouse Facility appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Richland Capital redeveloping 1984 office to 310K SF logistics at Tampa Lakeview Corporate Park
Property acquired 2023 for $10.97M; converted to two warehouse buildings with 32-foot clear heights
Office-to-industrial conversion signals weak Tampa office demand, strong logistics tailwinds
stories.summary: Richland Capital is converting a three-story 1984 office building into two rear-load logistics facilities totaling 310,000 square feet at Lakeview Corporate Park in Tampa. The company purchased the property in 2023 for $10.97 million and is now redeveloping it into a 106,375 SF and 203,320 SF warehouse with 32-foot clear heights to serve distribution and light industrial tenants. The project includes shared truck court and flexibility for build-to-suit and speculative office.
stories.impact: Office-to-industrial conversion reflects structural decline in Tampa office market and accelerating shift to logistics demand driven by port proximity and regional distribution needs. This suggests weak multifamily development feasibility in Tampa office-adjacent submarkets while signaling strong logistics-adjacent population growth (warehouse workers, logistics operations) that could anchor residential demand in emerging areas near I-75 corridors.

stories.title: Aerospace Firm Spending $75M on Orlando Plant
stories.excerpt: Extant Aerospace broke ground on a new 376,000-square-foot industrial manufacturing facility in Melbourne. Michael Moss II of Lightle Beckner Robison represented Extant Aerospace securing the building. The Orlando Business Journal reports the new facility is part of the Harbour Logistics Center development, an industrial center with a total of 530,000 square feet of Class-A space across two buildings. Extant will occupy “Building Two” with a secondary 154,000-square-foot space (known as Building 1) remaining available. Plans include a $75 million investment for the facility, as well as 200 new jobs added to Extant’s current 250-employee workforce This new facility will be an expansion from the company’s current 52,900-square-foot base at 1615 W. NASA Blvd. in Melbourne. Extant Aerospace works with a mix of aerospace electronics and avionics products used on a wide range of commercial and military platforms. The post Aerospace Firm Spending $75M on Orlando Plant appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Extant Aerospace investing $75M in 376K SF Melbourne facility; adding 200 jobs to 250-person workforce
Expansion from 52.9K SF current space; part of 530K SF Harbour Logistics Center development
Aerospace manufacturing growth signals skilled labor in-migration to Brevard County, Florida
stories.summary: Extant Aerospace broke ground on a $75 million, 376,000-square-foot manufacturing facility in Melbourne as part of the Harbour Logistics Center development. The facility represents an expansion from the company's current 52,900-square-foot operation and will add 200 new jobs to its existing 250-person workforce. Building Two will house Extant, with a 154,000-square-foot Building One remaining available for lease.
stories.impact: Aerospace manufacturing expansion adds 200 high-wage jobs to Melbourne/Brevard County, driving demographic tailwinds for multifamily demand in Space Coast corridor. Skilled aerospace workers typically earn $60K–$120K+ salaries, supporting Class A/B renter demand; timing and clustering of aerospace investment (SpaceX, Blue Origin proximity) suggests sustained job growth and in-migration window for acquisitions in Melbourne, Cocoa Beach, and Port St. Lucie submarkets.

stories.title: LBA Logistics Acquires 350K-SF Forsyth County Warehouse Pair
stories.excerpt: LBA Logistics recently purchased two industrial properties in the Union Cross Industrial Park near Winston-Salem for $38.16 million. The seller was Westcore. The Triad Business Journal reports Westcore purchased the two Millennium Drive properties from developer Front Street Capital for a combined $36.9 million in 2022. LBA purchased the industrial buildings at 4991 Millennium Drive and 4980 Millennium Drive for $21.8 million and $16.4 million, respectively. The 4991 Millennium Drive property contains a 200,000-square-foot building on a 21.5-acre site. Bunzl Distribution USA Inc. opened its $13.4 million plant at the site in 2019. 4980 Millennium Drive contains a 140,000-square-foot building on an 11.17-acre site. Fastener and Hooks manufacturer Southern Carlson leases the property. LBA focuses on the ownership and operation of industrial buildings across the country. LBA Logistics’ only other property in North Carolina is the RiverWest industrial building in Belmont, according to the portfolio on its website. The post LBA Logistics Acquires 350K-SF Forsyth County Warehouse Pair appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: LBA Logistics purchased 350K SF industrial pair in Union Cross/Winston-Salem for $38.16M from Westcore
Two buildings: 200K SF (Bunzl Distribution tenant) and 140K SF (Southern Carlson tenant); 32.67 acres
LBA expanding NC footprint; only prior holding was Belmont RiverWest asset
stories.summary: LBA Logistics acquired two industrial buildings totaling 350,000 square feet in Union Cross Industrial Park near Winston-Salem, North Carolina, for $38.16 million from Westcore. The 4991 Millennium Drive property (200K SF, occupied by Bunzl Distribution) sold for $21.8 million, while 4980 Millennium Drive (140K SF, leased to Southern Carlson) went for $16.4 million. This represents LBA's significant expansion into the Triad region, with the company previously operating only the RiverWest building in Belmont.
stories.impact: LBA's acquisition signals institutional capital confidence in Winston-Salem/Triad industrial market, but minimal direct multifamily impact. However, Westcore's exit and LBA's entry suggest industrial supply stabilization; watch for downstream mixed-use redevelopment at Union Cross as regional logistics hub matures, potentially freeing older land for multifamily conversion.

stories.title: Slate, RiseBoro Land $163M Loan for Two Williamsburg Housing Projects
stories.excerpt: Slate Property Group and RiseBoro Community Partnership have secured the financing to build 312 units of affordable housing in Williamsburg, Brooklyn. The joint venture led by the affordable housing development firm best known for its contribution to Steve Cohen’s redevelopment of Willets Point, Queens, closed on a $162.9 million loan to build two projects at 178 Montrose Avenue and the nearby 73 Meserole Street, according to the developers. The loan originated from J.P. Morgan Chase and was arranged by the New York City Housing Development Corporation (HDC) to facilitate the construction of 163 units at 178 Montrose and 149 units at 73 Meserole. About 60 percent of the units will be set aside for formerly homeless individuals. “Solving New York’s housing crisis means building affordable homes in every neighborhood, including high-cost communities where this housing is most needed but rarely built,” David Schwartz, co-founder of Slate Property Group, said in a statement. “As one of the city’s most expensive neighborhoods, Williamsburg has been out of reach for far too many working families.” The properties were purchased from Two Trees Management for only $2, with Slate and RiseBoro agreeing to let the Jed Walentas-led firm retain the air rights, according to the buyers. Rental rates at the development will range from $1,339 per month for a studio to $2,304 per month for a three-bedroom apartment, according to Slate and RiseBoro. Amenities will include outdoor and indoor recreation facilities, a fitness center and on-site laundry. Social services will also be available in the building with space for administrative support for residents. Schwartz and his co-founding partner Martin Nussbaum have launched affordable housing projects throughout the five boroughs, most notably in partnership with New York Mets owner Cohen and Hard Rock International to add 450 affordable housing units as a component to their casino development near Citi Field. Mark Hallum can be reached
bullets: Slate Property Group and RiseBoro closed $162.9M construction loan via JPMorgan/NYC HDC for Williamsburg
Two projects: 178 Montrose (163 units) and 73 Meserole (149 units); 312 total units, 60% set aside for formerly homeless
Affordable housing financing availability remains robust despite rate environment
stories.summary: Slate Property Group and RiseBoro Community Partnership closed a $162.9 million construction loan arranged by JPMorgan Chase and the NYC Housing Development Corporation to build 312 affordable units across two Williamsburg, Brooklyn properties. 178 Montrose Avenue will deliver 163 units and 73 Meserole Street will deliver 149 units, with approximately 60 percent designated for formerly homeless individuals. The financing underscores continued institutional and government support for affordable housing development in high-cost NYC markets.
stories.impact: Strong affordable housing construction financing in Williamsburg signals robust subsidy-backed capital availability but minimal spillover to market-rate multifamily development. For developers, this indicates continued competitive pressure from subsidized projects in desirable NYC submarkets and suggests mission-driven capital (HDC, JPMorgan CRA) may crowd out standard construction lending for market-rate projects in Williamsburg—target alternative submarkets (Sunset Park, Astoria, Long Island City) with stronger private capital flows.

stories.title: Charlotte’s Spectrum Companies Acquired by Lincoln Property Company
stories.excerpt: Dallas-based Lincoln Property Company (“Lincoln”), a global full-service real estate firm, acquired The Spectrum Companies (“Spectrum”), a Charlotte-based, full-service real estate firm. The acquisition enhances Lincoln’s full-service real estate platform in the Carolinas region, combining complementary capabilities, closely aligned cultures, and deep regional expertise. Johno Harris, Senior Executive Vice President at Lincoln and Stephen McClure, CEO of Spectrum will be leading Lincoln’s Carolinas platform, which will now also include Spectrum’s Northern and Central Florida’s multifamily business. Since 1982, Spectrum has developed nearly 7,000 multifamily units and currently leases and manages a total of 4.4 million square feet of assets, making this a significant expansion of Lincoln’s regional footprint. Spectrum will continue to build upon existing customer and investor relationships while expanding on the services and opportunities provided by incorporating Lincoln’s vertically integrated capabilities. This acquisition broadens Lincoln’s product offerings in the region. The post Charlotte’s Spectrum Companies Acquired by Lincoln Property Company appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Lincoln Property Company acquired Charlotte-based Spectrum Companies for undisclosed price
Spectrum has developed ~7K multifamily units since 1982; manages 4.4M SF assets across Carolinas and Central Florida
Consolidation expands Lincoln's regional platform; adds Spectrum's Florida multifamily operations
stories.summary: Dallas-based Lincoln Property Company acquired Charlotte-based Spectrum Companies, a full-service real estate firm that has developed nearly 7,000 multifamily units since 1982 and currently leases and manages 4.4 million square feet of assets. The acquisition strengthens Lincoln's platform in the Carolinas and adds Spectrum's Northern and Central Florida multifamily business, with executives Johno Harris and Stephen McClure leading the expanded region.
stories.impact: Acquisition signals consolidation of mid-market multifamily development and management platforms in Southeast, reducing fragmented regional competition. Lincoln gains immediate scale in Charlotte and Central Florida (high-growth markets); likely outcome is acceleration of Lincoln-branded development pipeline in both regions, potentially increased institutional capital deployment, and heightened competition for small-to-mid-sized land deals—watch for pre-emptive acquisitions of shovel-ready Spectrum-pipeline sites.

stories.title: New York’s Fair Fares Expansion Is a Win for Affordable Housing
stories.excerpt: In a city of straphangers, transportation is about far more than just moving people from Points A to B. It is the thread that ties everything together, connecting New Yorkers to their homes, education, health care, entertainment and nourishment. It is — quite literally — a path to a better future. Affordability is the key to making this complex ecosystem work. When transit is out of reach, both physically and financially, even the most cost-effective living situation can become a trap. Where you can afford to live should never determine whether you can reach opportunity. Yet, for too many New Yorkers, the cost of getting around has become a daily tax on working families. For households already spending half their income on rent, every fare matters. Carlina Rivera. Photo: Courtesy NYSASAF. That is why the historic expansion of the Fair Fares program in the city’s fiscal year 2027 adopted budget is more than just good transit policy. It is a direct investment in housing stability, economic mobility and a fairer New York. This year’s budget agreement added $54 million to the existing $120.6 million Fair Fares program, increasing eligibility for New York City families. Now an individual earning just under $32,000, or a family of four earning under $66,000, qualifies for half-price fares, a permanent increase from 150 percent to 200 percent of the federal poverty level — the largest expansion since the program began. The impact is staggering: 340,000 more New Yorkers will become eligible, raising total eligibility to approximately 1.3 million residents. For a low-income rider, that can mean saving up to $910 a year on transit — money that can go toward rent, food, medicine, child care or utilities. In a city where median asking rent has reached $4,120 — the highest on record since 2010 — those savings are critical. This matters deeply for the affordable housing industry because transportation and housing are deeply entwined. A home is not truly affordable if its resident
bullets: NYC expanded Fair Fares transit subsidy program by $54M in FY2027 budget; now totals $174.6M annually
Program increases eligibility for low-income NYC families; reduces effective cost of living
Transit subsidy lowers marginal cost of renting far from jobs, expanding affordable housing market geographically
stories.summary: New York City's FY2027 budget approved a $54 million increase to the Fair Fares program, bringing total annual funding to $174.6 million. The expansion increases subsidy eligibility for low-income families, reducing the transportation burden for working households already spending up to half their income on rent. The program effectively subsidizes transit access for income-qualified riders, enabling broader geographic distribution of housing choices.
stories.impact: Transit subsidies reduce effective rent-plus-commute costs for low-income renters, expanding feasible geographic range for affordable housing development beyond traditional central nodes. For multifamily developers, this creates opportunity windows in outer-borough and under-served transit-adjacent submarkets (Sunset Park, Astoria, Long Island City fringe areas, Harlem corridors) where lower land costs + Fair Fares subsidies = stronger renter demand and feasibility. Counterintuitively, widening affordable geography may reduce density incentives for dense, expensive central developments.

stories.title: Wholesale Inflation Follows Consumer Prices with June Decline
stories.excerpt: U.S. wholesale inflation followed the example of consumer price inflation in June with a monthly decline. The Producer Price Index for final demand fell by a seasonally adjusted 0.3% in June after rising 1.1% the previous month, the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics reported Wednesday. Analysts had expected the June figure to be in line with the May reading. On an unadjusted basis, the index for final demand increased 5.5% for the 12 months ended in June, representing a slowdown from the 6.5% annual increase reported in May. With food, energy and trade prices stripped out, the index held steady from the May figure of 5.1%. The June decline in the index for final demand can be attributed to prices for final demand goods, which fell 1.4%. At the same time, the index for final demand services moved up 0.2%. As with the Consumer Price Index, a drop in energy prices was a key factor in the June PPI results. Energy costs declined 6.4% for the month after rising 8.4% in May. The report follows Tuesday’s CPI, which showed consumer inflation decelerating to 3.5% year-over-year. The post Wholesale Inflation Follows Consumer Prices with June Decline appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: U.S. PPI fell 0.3% monthly in June 2024; 12-month PPI now 5.5%, down from 6.5% May
Energy prices drove decline (down 6.4% month, 8.4% in May); core services PPI held steady at 5.1%
Wholesale inflation decelerating alongside consumer CPI (3.5% year-over-year)
stories.summary: U.S. wholesale inflation (Producer Price Index) declined 0.3 percent on a seasonally adjusted basis in June 2024, with the 12-month PPI slowing to 5.5 percent from 6.5 percent in May. Energy prices fell 6.4 percent for the month, a major driver of the decline. Core PPI (excluding food, energy, and trade services) held steady at 5.1 percent, while consumer-facing services PPI rose 0.2 percent.
stories.impact: Decelerating wholesale inflation signals potential construction cost relief for multifamily developers, particularly in materials and energy-intensive components (steel, concrete, HVAC). Soft PPI may support cap-rate compression and improved underwriting viability in coming quarters if trend persists. However, sticky core services inflation (labor, design, permitting) suggests hard costs may not fall as much as input costs—expect continued pressure on labor-dependent trade work and subcontractor margins through 2024–2025.

stories.title: The Plan: Major League Soccer Scores Big With Global HQ Design at Vornado’s 2 Penn
stories.excerpt: For many people, their love of soccer — or football, as the rest of the world calls it — came from running around the neighborhood as a kid, kicking a ball into a makeshift net while excitedly yelling “goooooooal” with friends. That idea of community and neighborhood connectivity is baked into the newly opened Major League Soccer (MLS) headquarters at Vornado Realty Trust’s 2 Penn Plaza. Actually, the new HQ has that sense of community plus a bright and bold stadium experience unlike any other office environment in Midtown Manhattan. For those two drastically different dynamics to coexist across a two-floor, 126,000-square-foot space, TPG Architecture took inspiration from the game itself. “It all tied into the essence of how we communicated the quality of the sport in architectural language,” said Chelsea Rowe, creative director at TPG Architecture. “With soccer, there’s peaks and crescendos, bursts of activity, moments of flair, moments of greatness, but there are also moments of steady rhythm between that.” When you get off the elevator on the 15th floor of 2 Penn, the immediate feeling is that you have arrived at a major event. It’s a little surprising that “Ya’ll ready for this” isn’t playing at full blast when the doors open. The elevator corridor that goes to the MLS reception area suggests a stadium players tunnel and features the bright red MLS brand above your head as you walk into work. Although, if you have a big marketing meeting with another major brand, that MLS logo at the elevator bank can be changed to match the colors of the corporation you’re meeting — perhaps Caterpillar yellow or Walmart blue. The tunnel leads to an open reception space that features an abundance of light and is infused with technology, including screens showing games, digital scoreboards and a ticker circling the staircase that leads down into the massive communal space called “Midfield.” On display in the reception area is a players’ jersey wall and the MLS Cup, the trophy aw
bullets: Major League Soccer relocated global HQ to Vornado's 2 Penn Plaza, Midtown Manhattan; 126K SF two-floor space
Office occupancy signal: major sports/media tenant commitment to Midtown, counters flight-to-suburbs narrative
Limited direct multifamily impact; signals corporate office recovery in Manhattan CBD
stories.summary: Major League Soccer opened its new global headquarters at Vornado Realty Trust's 2 Penn Plaza in Midtown Manhattan, occupying 126,000 square feet across two floors with a stadium-inspired design by TPG Architecture. The space features a jersey wall, MLS Cup display, and tech-forward office environment designed to reflect community connectivity and the sport's dynamism. The lease represents a vote of confidence in Midtown office recovery.
stories.impact: MLS HQ commitment to 2 Penn provides modest support for Midtown Manhattan office market recovery, reducing negative spillover to adjacent multifamily submarkets. Limited direct demand signal for apartments (MLS employees likely already Manhattan-based or relocating from elsewhere); however, office recovery reduces conversion pressure on older office stock, preserving development option value for future office-to-residential conversions if market swings.

stories.title: PTM Partners, Peacock launch subsidy-free workforce housing platform
stories.excerpt: Florida-based PTM Partners and Peacock Capital launched Inception Housing, a platform designed to deliver lower-cost housing without relying on government aid.  The venture targets middle-income renters earning between 80 and 120 percent of the area median income, cutting development costs by handling much of the design, construction and material procurement in-house, according to a news release.  By using a standardized building design and cold-formed steel purchased directly from suppliers, Inception Housing can cut hard construction costs by about 25 percent compared to similar products, PTM Partners CEO Michael Tillman said. The platform’s strategy delivers buildings in six to seven months, […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: PTM Partners and Peacock Capital launched Inception Housing platform targeting 80–120% AMI renters
Technology cuts hard costs ~25%, reduces construction time 50% (6–7 months vs. 12–14 months); uses cold-formed steel, standardized design
First project: Magnolia Flats in South Orlando (88 total units); South Florida faces 30K+ unit shortage
stories.summary: Florida-based PTM Partners and Peacock Capital launched Inception Housing, a platform designed to deliver workforce housing (80–120 percent AMI) without government subsidies by cutting hard construction costs approximately 25 percent and reducing build time to 6–7 months versus traditional 12–14 months. The platform uses standardized designs and direct procurement of cold-formed steel. The first project, Magnolia Flats in South Orlando, will deliver 88 units across redeveloped townhomes and new construction on vacant land.
stories.impact: Cost-reduction innovation (25% hard cost savings + 50% faster delivery) meaningfully improves feasibility for market-rate workforce housing at 80–120% AMI in South Florida's expensive markets. This addresses structural supply gap (30K+ units needed regionally) and could accelerate pipeline for workforce housing in secondary Florida markets (Orlando, Tampa, Jacksonville) where land costs are lower but labor and material costs remain elevated. Watch for rapid scaling; if replicable, Inception disrupts traditional development economics and may force competitors to consolidate or specialize.

stories.title: Developer Wolfe Landau Buys FiDi Redevelopment Site for $35M
stories.excerpt: An entity tied to Brooklyn-based developer Wolfe Landau has snapped up two adjacent Financial District lots poised for redevelopment. The buyer, Landau’s Peninim Water, paid $35 million across two deals for 78 Pearl Street and 46-48 Water Street, according to property records made public Tuesday. The New York Business Journal first reported the deal. The block-through, irregularly-shaped site between Coenties Slip and Hanover Square spans a little over 10,000 square feet. It includes a vacant lot along Pearl Street and an empty, mixed-use building on Water Street. The latter, built in 1929, spans roughly 86,000 square feet. The Pearl and Water street addresses were advertised exclusively by Bob Knakal’s BKREA as “a prime mixed-use development or conversion/enlargement opportunity.” It is unclear, however, what exactly Landau plans to do with the purchase. Spokespeople for Landau’s Watermark Capital Group and BKREA did not respond to a request for comment. Records from the New York City Department of Buildings indicate the Water Street property is being prepared for demolition, the NYBJ reported. The selling entities were the Milstein Organization, commercial real estate firm Swig Company and Weiler Arnow Management. The Milstein Organization, a family-owned firm and operator of Milstein Properties, has owned the pair of properties since the 1970s and `80s, according to public records. The Milstein Organization could not be immediately reached for comment. Emily Davis can be reached at edavis@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Wolfe Landau acquired two adjacent Financial District lots (78 Pearl, 46–48 Water Street) for $35M
Site spans 10K+ SF; includes vacant lot and 86K SF mixed-use building (1929, scheduled for demolition)
Purchase signals developer confidence in Lower Manhattan redevelopment despite murky end-use strategy
stories.summary: Brooklyn-based developer Wolfe Landau, through entity Peninim Water, purchased two adjacent Financial District properties—78 Pearl Street and 46–48 Water Street—for $35 million total. The combined site spans just over 10,000 square feet and includes a vacant lot on Pearl Street and a vacant mixed-use building on Water Street (built 1929, 86K SF) slated for demolition. The exact redevelopment strategy remains unclear; the site was marketed as a prime mixed-use development or conversion opportunity.
stories.impact: Lower Manhattan redevelopment acquisition signals developer conviction in FiDi recovery post-pandemic office decline. Site size (10K SF) and location suggest potential residential conversion/new construction play, but insufficient detail to determine multifamily vs. mixed-use vs. hospitality thesis. Monitor: if Landau pursues residential, it signals belief in downtown Manhattan renter demand rebound and could validate similar FiDi acquisitions by other developers; if hospitality/commercial, reflects continued office pressure.

stories.title: London Nightclub Operator Buys 675 Hudson Street for $100M
stories.excerpt: After unveiling plans for a rooftop nightclub at the 14th Street Triangle, London-based club venue operator Caprice Holdings has bought the property for $100 million, according to property records made public Tuesday. Robert Cayre’s Aurora Capital Associates sold the five-story building at 675 Hudson Street to Caprice, which plans to alter the 19th century property — if the New York City Landmarks Preservation Commission (LPC) signs off on the work — and turn it into the New York location for a private club known in London as Annabel’s. The buyer’s address on the deed in property records is tied to Caprice, which is a sister brand of Annabel’s. Both brands were previously under British businessman Richard Caring‘s hospitality empire, but were acquired by Abu Dhabi-based firm DIAFA earlier this year. Caprice secured $165 million in financing from HSBC for its new purchase, according to property records. Aurora acquired the Meatpacking District property in a 2023 bargain sale for $50 million, a deed transfer indicates. It’s unclear who brokered the sale. Aurora and Caprice did not immediately respond to requests for comment. A previous LPC hearing saw agency officials express reservations about the rooftop addition to the landmarked Hudson Street property, stating that the proposed curved glass structure designed by BKSK Architects did not match the character of the building. Comments in the margins of the latest presentation to the LPC recommend that the new owners “improve [the] design relationship between rooftop addition and underlying historic building.” Other comments noted that a “curvilinear form of rooftop addition is fighting geometry of historic building; scale should be reduced.” 675 Hudson is also known as the Herring Building. Construction began in 1849 with further additions throughout the next half century, and it originally served as a factory making masonry supplies. Mark Hallum can be reached at mhallum@commercialobserver.com
bullets: Caprice Holdings acquired 675 Hudson Street, Meatpacking District, for $100M; secured $165M HSBC financing
Property purchased from Aurora Capital (who bought 2023 for $50M); planned conversion to Annabel's private club/nightclub
Nightclub-focused acquisition diverts asset from multifamily potential; limited residential impact
stories.summary: London-based Caprice Holdings purchased the five-story building at 675 Hudson Street in the Meatpacking District for $100 million and secured $165 million in HSBC financing. The company, which owns the Annabel's brand (recently acquired by Abu Dhabi's DIAFA), plans to convert the 19th-century property into a New York location for the members-only private club, subject to Landmarks Preservation Commission approval. Aurora Capital Associates, the prior owner, had acquired the property in a 2023 bargain sale for $50 million.
stories.impact: Nightclub-focused capital deployment at premium Hudson Street location ($100M for single five-story building) signals extremely tight high-end Meatpacking District real estate market but removes asset from potential multifamily redevelopment. This represents loss of optionality for residential conversion; accumulation of such specialist uses (clubs, hotels, retail flagships) in Meatpacking may suppress multifamily development feasibility by shrinking available development sites and elevating land basis for non-residential uses.

stories.title: Dallas apartment pipeline gets a boost with pair of financing deals
stories.excerpt: Two developers of a pair of apartment complexes in the Dallas-Fort Worth metroplex recently secured financing for their separate projects.  Endeavor Real Estate locked in financing for their luxury high-rise at 2700 McKinney Avenue named Lucille. Additionally, StreetLights Residential snagged $54 million in equity financing for an apartment tower in Plano, Texas, which will be built in the redevelopment of The Park at Legacy at 6501 Legacy Drive.  Lucille will feature 265 units across 22 stories in Uptown, according to the Dallas Morning News. The development is helmed by Austin-based Endeavor and equity partner Canyon Partners Real Estate. Construction on […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Endeavor Real Estate secured financing for Lucille, 265-unit luxury high-rise at 2700 McKinney, Dallas Uptown
StreetLights Residential raised $54M equity for 22-story tower in Plano (Park at Legacy redevelopment, 6501 Legacy Drive)
Two financing wins boost Dallas-Fort Worth multifamily pipeline; construction to begin summer 2024, delivery 2028
stories.summary: Two major Dallas-Fort Worth apartment financings closed: Endeavor Real Estate locked in construction financing for Lucille, a 265-unit luxury high-rise at 2700 McKinney Avenue in Uptown Dallas (22 stories, 320K SF with 10K SF retail, rooftop pool, observation deck, four-level underground parking). StreetLights Residential secured $54 million in equity financing for a 22-story apartment tower in Plano as part of The Park at Legacy redevelopment (107-acre mixed-use campus). Lucille construction begins summer 2024 with leasing expected late 2028.
stories.impact: Twin financing approvals signal strong capital appetite for Class A multifamily in Dallas-Fort Worth, particularly high-rise luxury (Uptown CBD) and mixed-use redevelopment (Plano office-to-residential conversion). Capital availability supports pipeline acceleration in two key DFW submarkets; however, both projects target high-barrier segments (Uptown office workers, legacy-park-adjacent affluent renters), leaving secondary/workforce markets underserved. Expect continued flight to highest-density/highest-income submarkets; opportunity exists in emerging nodes (Frisco, Addison, Carrollton) with less institutional capital focus.

stories.title: Texas’ top construction permits for the week ending July 14, 2026
stories.excerpt: New Construction Fort Worth’s public hospital John Peter Smith Hospital filed the state’s biggest construction project of the week to build a $300 million central utility plant at 1575 South Main Street on Fort Worth’s South Side. Construction on the 113,000-square-foot facility started at the end of June and is expected to wrap up in May 2028.  Job filing number: TABS2026024842 Houston Independent School District filed plans to build a 216,000-square-foot career and technical center at 9220 Jutland Road in Houston. Construction on the project, which will feature classrooms, labs, a multipurpose center, kitchen and administration spaces, is expected to […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Texas' largest permit week: $300M Fort Worth hospital utility plant (113K SF, ends May 2028); $98M Amazon Terrell distribution center (1.2M SF)
Houston ISD career/technical center (216K SF, starts March 2027); Dallas Museum of Art mechanical upgrade ($20M, 20K SF)
Mix of industrial, institutional, cultural; no major residential permits listed in weekly top projects
stories.summary: Texas' largest construction permits for week ending July 14, 2026 include a $300 million John Peter Smith Hospital central utility plant in Fort Worth (113K SF, June 2026–May 2028), a $98 million Amazon distribution center in Terrell (1.2M SF, one-year build from August 2026), a Houston ISD career/technical center (216K SF, March 2027–September 2028), Dallas Museum of Art mechanical systems upgrade ($20M, 20K SF), and an Austin tech tenant improvement for Mission 42 Ventures at Shoal Creek Walk ($14M, 26K SF). No major multifamily projects topped the weekly permit list.
stories.impact: Amazon logistics facility (1.2M SF) in Terrell signals continued supply chain decentralization and warehousing density in Dallas exurbs, supporting secondary market population in-migration and worker housing demand. Hospital utility plant and institutional/cultural projects suggest public-sector capex, not directly residential-supportive but indicate sustained economic activity. Absence of major residential permits in top weekly list suggests multifamily development either underway (not newly permitted) or facing permitting delays/entitlement constraints—implies potential supply gaps and rent pressure building in DFW outside top office/industrial nodes.

stories.title: Dolphins out, superyachts in: David Martin’s marina plan at former Seaquarium getting underway
stories.excerpt: The site of the former Miami Seaquarium on Virginia Key is headed for a major redevelopment.  Miami-Dade County and Terra Group’s David Martin want to trade dolphin shows for superyachts with an application to build what could be the largest marina in the county, the Miami Herald reported.  The application, submitted to state regulators, calls for 325 boat slips, about 90 of which could accommodate yachts longer than 80 feet, and a 500-boat dry stack, the outlet said. The plan includes a fuel depot, a network of floating docks that extends halfway across the channel separating Virginia Key from nearby […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Terra Group (David Martin) pursuing 4400 Rickenbacker Causeway (former Miami Seaquarium) for 325-slip marina plus 500-boat dry stack
Miami-Dade owns land; negotiating ground lease with Martin (95% profit-split to Martin); FDEP flagged wetlands/water quality concerns
Hospitality/marine-focused redevelopment; minimal multifamily impact but signals Miami waterfront redevelopment momentum
stories.summary: Terra Group's David Martin submitted plans to redevelop the former Miami Seaquarium site at 4400 Rickenbacker Causeway into a large-scale marina with 325 boat slips (90+ for superyachts 80+ feet), 500-boat dry stack, fuel depot, floating docks, wave break fishing pier, and 19K SF fuel dock. Miami-Dade County, which owns the land and evicted Seaquarium in 2024, is negotiating a ground lease with Martin that would give him 95 percent of boat slip profits. The Florida Department of Environmental Protection has raised concerns about dredging, wetland impacts, and water quality management.
stories.impact: Marina redevelopment diverts premium Miami waterfront acreage from potential multifamily development and signals specialized marine/hospitality focus rather than housing. However, approval of large-scale Rickenbacker Causeway project would validate broader Virginia Key redevelopment momentum and may unlock adjacent residential parcels; watch for downstream hotel, mixed-use, or residential conversions on freed non-marina parcels as waterfront anchors (parks, restaurants, entertainment) cluster. Minimal near-term multifamily demand signal; potential medium-term positive spillover if marina becomes destination amenity.

stories.title: TIF funding for the 78 gets key approval for $425M for needed infrastructure
stories.excerpt: The Chicago City Council Committee on Finance approved a deal to fund $425 million of infrastructure at the 78, a mixed-use development that will include a privately-funded new stadium for the Major League Soccer franchise the Chicago Fire.  The deal would repay developer Related Midwest for project-supporting structures using tax increment financing money, including $287 million in TIF money from a nearby district. The next step in the deal will be getting the formal green light from the City Council, who are set to hold a final vote on the agreement on Wednesday, according to Crain’s.  Joe Mansueto, the Fire’s […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Chicago City Council Finance Committee approved $425M TIF funding for the 78 mixed-use soccer stadium development by Related Midwest
Deal repays Related $287M from Canal/Congress TIF district plus $425M total infrastructure; final City Council vote Wednesday
TIF mechanism enables large public subsidy for mixed-use development; signals multifamily-supportive public capital
stories.summary: Chicago City Council Committee on Finance approved a $425 million infrastructure funding package for the 78, a mixed-use development by Related Midwest anchored by a new Major League Soccer stadium. The deal redirects $287 million from the Canal/Congress TIF district to the Roosevelt/Clark TIF district (where the 78 is located) to repay Related for project-supporting infrastructure work. Final City Council vote is scheduled for Wednesday. The financing structure allows Related to defer immediate tax-revenue generation while infrastructure is built out.
stories.impact: TIF-funded infrastructure at the 78 creates favorable development economics for Related's broader mixed-use buildout, including multifamily components. TIF mechanism effectively subsidizes ancillary residential development (parking, streets, utilities) that improves project-level returns and anchors longer-term leasing demand via sports/entertainment tenancy. However, Alderman Conway's concern about draining Canal/Congress TIF signals political tension; watch for future TIF-backed projects to face tighter scrutiny. For developers outside the 78, TIF competition may tighten, suggesting focus on self-supporting projects in non-TIF submarkets (Pilsen, Logan Square, West Loop).

stories.title: Housing Notes: Developers are in on the joke: Apologize later
stories.excerpt: I have long had a fascination with the grit it takes to be a Manhattan developer. After all, I taught market analysis to a class of approximately 150 graduate students in Columbia’s Master of Science in Real Estate Development (MSRED) program for 8 years. My students have ended up all over the U.S. and hopefully they learned not to accept “no” as an answer when they’re out in the world. That’s the dividing line since developers tend to ignore the word “no” and keep pushing, when non-developers like me would cower in fear and self-doubt. It helps to look at […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Commentary on Manhattan developer persistence (Harry Macklowe midnight demolitions); Nathan Berman's MetroLoft Pfizer HQ conversion suffered partial collapse
Observation on developer personality/risk tolerance; office-to-residential conversion risk highlighted
Philosophical piece on developer behavior; limited quantifiable market signal
stories.summary: Real estate analyst reflects on Manhattan developer mindset and persistence, citing developer tendency to ignore regulatory obstacles and restart ventures after failures. Essay highlights the Pfizer headquarters conversion by MetroLoft's Nathan Berman (considered an atypical 'quiet' developer), which recently suffered a partial building collapse—an outlier among 'personality-disorder' developers noted by appraiser Jonathan Miller. Commentary suggests developers typically cycle through boom-bust-restart patterns.
stories.impact: Office-to-residential conversion risks are real and material, as evidenced by MetroLoft's structural failure. For multifamily developers considering large-scale office conversions (particularly older Manhattan office buildings), this case study underscores need for rigorous structural assessment, load-bearing remediation planning, and contingency capital. No direct market-level impact, but serves as cautionary signal on conversion feasibility and hidden cost overruns in trophy/legacy office assets.
